name: Computed Fields vs Transclusions: Gap Analysis

content.description:
**Status:** Assessment (March 2026) An analysis of what [Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000) and [Structured Notes](item://c0c0c0c0-0073-0000-0000-000000000000) provide compared to the existing [Transclusion](item://c0c0c0c0-0035-0000-0000-000000000000) system, and what gaps must be closed before transclusions could be removed. ## What Transclusions Provide Today The transclusion system (`![](item://...)` in [hobson-markdown](item://a1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-e1f2a3b4c5d6)) provides: | Feature | Syntax | |---------|--------| | Full item rendering | `![](item://id)` — renders entire item via `renderItem()` | | Field extraction | `![](item://id#description)` — pulls a specific field | | Region extraction | `![](item://id#code?region=name)` — named `[BEGIN:]/[END:]` regions | | Line range | `![](item://id#code?lines=10-20)` | | Symbol extraction | `![](item://id?symbol=fnName)` — resolves via `_symbols` metadata | | Render mode override | `?render=markdown` or `?render=code` | | Functional transclusion | `![](item://id?call=fn)` — calls module export, returns DOM or string | | Query blocks | ` ```query ``` ` — arbitrary JS with `api`, `transclude()`, `transcludeEach()` | | Collapsible chrome | `
` box with "Transcluded from" header and navigation link | | Editor gesture | "Insert Link/Transclusion" toolbar button with item search modal | ## What Computed Fields Cover The computed fields system (`content.computed` + `{{field-name}}`) covers: | Feature | Equivalent | |---------|------------| | Field transclusion | `(get-field @other :description)` | | Functional transclusion | `(invoke (require "module") "fn" api params)` | | Computed values | Any Hob expression — arithmetic, aggregation, etc. | | Cross-item reactivity | Automatic re-render when referenced items change | | Cycle detection | `_computingFields` Set prevents infinite recursion | | Error sentinels | Graceful `ERR` display with tooltip, error items via `captureError` | | Provenance links | AST walk extracts `@`-referenced items in field summary | | Inline rendering | `{{field-name}}` in markdown body with dashed underline, clickable | Computed fields are stronger than transclusions in reactivity, error handling, and extensibility to non-transclusion use cases (spreadsheet calculations, aggregations). But they don't cover everything. ## Gaps ### 1. Full item rendering — no equivalent Transclusion renders an *entire item* via `renderItem()` inside a `
` box — the full view, not just a single field. Computed fields can only return a single field value. There's no `{{}}` equivalent for "embed this item's complete rendered view here." **Possible fix:** A Hob function like `(render-item @other-item)` that returns a DOM element. The functional transclusion path already supports DOM returns, so this may be straightforward. ### 2. Region extraction (`?region=name`) — no equivalent Transclusions can pull a named `[BEGIN:name]/[END:name]` region from a code field. `get-field` returns the entire field value. **Possible fix:** A stdlib function `(extract-region (get-field @item :code) "regionName")`. ### 3. Line range extraction (`?lines=10-20`) — no equivalent Extracting a slice of a multiline string. **Possible fix:** `(slice-lines (get-field @item :code) 10 20)`. ### 4. Symbol extraction (`?symbol=fnName`) — no equivalent Transclusions resolve symbols via `_symbols` metadata to extract function/class definitions. No Hob function exposes this. **Possible fix:** `(extract-symbol @item "fnName")` that reads `_symbols` and applies the line range. ### 5. Query blocks — no equivalent ` ```query ``` ` blocks execute arbitrary JS with full `api` access and return DOM or markdown. Computed fields use Hob and are per-field, not inline blocks. The `transclude()` and `transcludeEach()` helpers for attribution chrome have no Hob equivalents. This is the biggest gap. Query blocks enable dynamic lists, filtered views, and aggregation dashboards with flexible output formatting. Computed fields can do *some* of this via `(reduce + 0 (map ...))`, but can't easily produce complex multi-item markdown with attribution chrome. This may need to remain as a separate mechanism, or evolve into a Hob equivalent. ### 6. Collapsible chrome with provenance — partially covered Transclusions render in collapsible `
` boxes with "Transcluded from: [Name] ↗" headers. Computed field `{{refs}}` render inline with dashed underlines and click-to-scroll — no visual boxing, no collapse, no "from" attribution at the reference site. Provenance is only shown in the field summary section at the bottom. This is a deliberate design choice (inline vs. block), but for cases where the user wants to embed a paragraph from another note with visible attribution, the old chrome is more appropriate. ### 7. Editor gesture for computed transclusions — not implemented The old editor has an "Insert Link/Transclusion" button that opens a search modal and inserts `![name](item://id)` at the cursor. The [Structured Notes](item://c0c0c0c0-0073-0000-0000-000000000000) doc describes a gesture that creates both the `content.computed` entry and the `{{name}}` reference at the cursor, but this isn't built yet. Without this, creating a computed-field transclusion requires: (1) switch to edit mode, (2) add a computed field in the computed-fields section, (3) write the formula manually, (4) go back to the body and type `{{name}}`. Four steps vs. one click. ### 8. Render mode override — no equivalent `?render=code` forces code-block rendering of a description field. Computed fields return raw values — the rendering is always inline text. ## Inline-Editable Field References ### The Vision Today, `{{field-name}}` references are always readonly — changing a field value requires switching to edit mode and finding the field in the static-fields or computed-fields section. If `{{field-name}}` could render as an editable input, structured notes would feel like spreadsheets: click on a value, type a new one, and watch computed fields reactively update. For example, a note with `The total is **{{total}}**` where `total` is computed from `{{quantity!}} × {{unit-price!}}` — you'd click on "10", type "12", and see `total` update immediately. This also opens the door to **form-like notes**: `Name: {{name!}}, Email: {{email!}}, Status: {{status!}}` where each field is directly editable inline. ### Syntax: `{{field-name!}}` The note author controls which references are editable via a `!` suffix: - `{{field-name}}` — readonly (default, backwards compatible) - `{{field-name!}}` — editable inline The `!` follows the Hob convention for mutation (like `set!`). The author's intent is visible right where the reference appears — no need to look in a separate config map. The parser change is minimal: `resolveRefsMarkup` already extracts the field name from between `{{` and `}}`; it would trim a trailing `!` and pass an `editable` flag to the rendering logic. **Future extension:** If richer per-field config is needed later (input type, placeholder, validation, choices), a `content.fields` metadata map could provide defaults, with `!` as sugar for `{ "editable": true }`. But for the initial implementation, the syntax alone is sufficient. ### Rendering `resolveRefsMarkup` currently generates HTML strings injected into markdown before markdown-it processes it. For editable fields, it would use the **DOM mount pattern** (already used for DOM-node computed field values): insert a `` placeholder in the HTML string, then mount a real interactive element after markdown rendering. **Display mode:** The field shows as text with visual affordances distinguishing it from readonly refs — a solid underline (vs. dashed for readonly/computed), `cursor: text`, and a subtle background tint on hover. **Click:** Text becomes an `` (or `
